questions.prompt: Right approach for migrating a CSS Modules codebase to Tailwind?

choices.text: Incremental migration over months. Install Tailwind alongside existing CSS; disable Tailwind's preflight if it conflicts with your reset; write all NEW components in Tailwind from day one; convert existing components opportunistically when feature work touches them. After 6-12 months, most code is Tailwind and a focused sprint cleans up the rest. Big-bang rewrites almost always fail — too risky, too long, too much regression area, team morale erodes. Both can coexist for months without problems; pragmatism beats purity during migration. Set a goal (e.g., '5 components per sprint converted') and progress accumulates.

questions.explanation: Incremental; new code → Tailwind; existing → convert when touched. Big-bang fails.

questions.prompt: After installing Tailwind alongside existing CSS, the legacy site looks different — margins changed, default styles shifted. Likely cause?

choices.text: Tailwind's preflight (CSS reset) conflicts with the legacy reset or default browser styles your site was relying on. Preflight zeroes out margins on headings, normalizes form elements, etc. — usually good but disruptive to established designs that depended on the previous defaults. Two fixes: (1) Disable preflight via `corePlugins: { preflight: false }` in tailwind.config — recommended for established codebases. The legacy reset/defaults remain; Tailwind utilities still work. Re-enable preflight later when fully migrated. (2) Audit specific conflicts and add resets back where needed. Disabling is usually simpler and lower-risk for migration.

questions.explanation: Tailwind's preflight (reset) conflicts with legacy defaults. Disable via corePlugins for migration.
